data.title: Stocks end higher as Powell soothes economic worries, but post sharp weekly loss
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages closed higher Friday, aided by Fed Chairman Jerome Powell's economic reassurance, but stocks sank for the week as investors wrestled with tariff twists from the Trump administration. 

The "U.S. economy continues to be in a good place. The labor market is solid, and inflation has moved closer to our 2 percent longer-run goal," Powell said in a speech Friday afternoon. 

Stocks turned higher, leaving the S&P 500 500 (SP500) +0.6% at 5,770.20. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.7%, and the Dow (DJI) +0.5%. The Nasdaq-100 (NDX) +0.7%. It was on the edge of a correction, down 9% from its latest peak.  

Stocks slid for the week. The S&P 500 (SP500) slumped 3.1%, its steepest loss since early September. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -3.5%, and the Dow (DJI) -2.4%.

"Q1 weakness continues in equities," with the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and the S&P 500 (SP500) each hovering around their 200-day moving averages, Alex King, investing group leader of Cestrian Capital Research, told Seeking Alpha.

"This is an area where we may see the indices find support. The proof will be when the 200-day averages are re-tested from above and hold fast," King said. "We look to next week to see if any such rally can sustain in volume". 

Bond yields flipped up. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.32%, as prices fell. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 4%.

Stocks fell sharply earlier Friday after February nonfarm payrolls rose less than expected. On the tariff front, U.S. President Donald Trump said Canadian lumber and dairy products could face reciprocal tariffs. 

"The week confirmed that tariffs remain a wildcard for market confidence, and traders are exhausted from the back-and-forth," Mark Malek, chief investment officer at Siebert Financial, said in a note. Markets have moved "painfully in the red" even after Trump delayed some tariffs for a month, Malek said.

The jobs report showed additions of +151K vs. 160K consensus, and average wage growth increasing 0.3% but cooling from January. 

The "markets should breathe a sigh of relief that there wasn’t a shock in either direction and the report was a mixed bag. The takeaway is that wage data declined which is disinflationary," Gina Bolvin, president of Bolvin Wealth Management Group, said. 

Traders raised the odds of a June interest rate cut by the Fed, paring odds for a May meeting reduction. 

In equities, HP Enterprise (HPE) -11.9% following a lower-than expected outlook stemming from tariffs and competitive pricing. 

Broadcom (AVGO) +8.7% after the semiconductor and software giant reported first-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations.

data.title: Rail Vision receives Nasdaq non-compliance notification
data.description:
• Rail Vision (NASDAQ:RVSN) said on Friday it has received a written notice from Nasdaq indicating that the company was not in compliance with listing rule, as the company’s closing bid price for its ordinary shares was below $1.00 per share for the last 30 consecutive business days.
  • The company has been granted a 180-calendar day compliance period, or until September 2, 2025, to regain compliance with the minimum bid price requirement.
  • If the company is not in compliance by September 2, 2025, the company may be afforded a second 180-calendar day compliance period.
  • The company intends to monitor the closing bid price of its ordinary shares between now and September 2, 2025, and will consider available options to resolve the company’s noncompliance with the minimum bid price requirement as may be necessary.

data.title: Janus International pays down $40M debt
data.description:
• Janus International Group (NYSE:JBI) has paid down $40M in debt using cash on hand, the company said on Friday. The $40 million was paid against the company’s first lien term loan facility.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Rio Tinto scraps plan to raise up to $5B in share sale - Bloomberg
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) has ended plans to raise as much as $5B in a share sale following pushback from investors, Bloomberg reported Friday.

Rio (NYSE:RIO) executives had floated the possibility of an equity offering in recent investor meetings, but Rio reportedly decided to shelve the idea after getting significant pushback, especially as the company did not think the offering was a financial necessity.

The fundraising could have been used to help pay for Rio's (RIO) $6.7B acquisition of Arcadium Lithium as well as rebalance its shareholding between U.K. and Australian investors, according to the report.

The capital raise also would have added more liquidity among its Australian shareholders, as its share register is much more weighted to London, with ~75% of its stock there.

Rio (RIO) completed its Arcadium takeover earlier this week, and said it was funding the deal by drawing on an existing bridge loan facility, which it plans to replace with long-term debt financing.

data.title: Trump kicks off first-ever White House crypto summit with industry titans
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Friday kicked off the White House's first-ever crypto-focused summit, joined by a roster of high-profile industry executives like Strategy (MSTR) Co-Founder Michael Saylor, MARA (MARA) CEO Fred Thiel and Coinbase (COIN) CEO Brian Armstrong.

In the run-up to the summit, Trump signed a long-sought executive order Thursday evening establishing a national bitcoin (BTC-USD) reserve. Many had expected the announcement to come during Friday's event. Still, J.P. Morgan analysts laid out why crypto may remain under pressure over the near term.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Canada's storied Hudson Bay nearing bankruptcy - WSJ
data.description:
Hudson Bay, which got its start in the 17th century fur trade, is reportedly nearing bankruptcy, sources told The Wall Street Journal.

The storied Canadian department store, known for its iconic striped blankets and high-end apparel, began operations in Winnipeg, Canada, and is now in 86 locations across Canada.

In 2008 the company was sold to private-equity firm and Lord & Taylor-owner, NRDC equity partners with the Hudson Bay Company brand affiliated with other department stores including Saks Fifth Avenue, Bergdorf Goodman, and Neiman Marcus.

Hudson Bay eventually became a stand-alone business in 2024 following a deal to spin off Saks Fifth Avenue with Neiman Marcus, creating Saks Global. The affiliated businesses are not part of an expected bankruptcy filing.

Hudson Bay department stores currently sit on some of the most prime real estate in Canada, including 844K square feet in British Columbia, 655K square feet in downtown Montreal, and its 850K flagship store on Toronto’s Queen Street.

Unfortunately, the lucrative real estate could not compensate for the company’s missteps including an outdated retail model, the Saks separation, and a competitive landscape that has seen rivals offer more affordably priced apparel. In addition, the challenging retail environment that has plagued other department stores since the COVID pandemic left Hudson Bay with mounting debt that forced the company to close stores and conduct widespread layoffs.  

Hudson Bay Corporation is expected to file bankruptcy “within days” sources told The Wall Street Journal.

Related tickers: Macy’s (NYSE:M), Dillard’s (NYSE:DDS), Nordstrom (NYSE:JWN), Kohl’s (NYSE:KSS)

data.title: Hawaiian Electric upgraded by Evercore on increased confidence in settlement
data.description:
Hawaiian Electric (NYSE:HE) +11.5% in Friday's trading to a YTD high after Evercore ISI upgrades shares to Outperform from In-Line with a $14 price target, raised from $12, believing the market underappreciates the Hawaii Supreme Court decision that lowers the likelihood that insurers would disrupt the settlement.

Evercore ISI's Michael Lonegan and Durgesh Chopra say Hawaiian Electric (NYSE:HE) is now better positioned than when it previously traded at a comparable valuation vs. peers, citing the definitive wildfire settlement agreement, improved balance sheet with appropriate proceeds from the American Savings Bank sale directed towards debt reduction, strong access to capital markets, significantly higher liquidity, and potential legislation support.

The progression of legislative efforts including SB-897 and HB-982 signals backing for a wildfire insurance fund, securitization, and liability limits, the analysts say, adding that a finalized settlement and favorable legislation could trigger multiple upgrades, eventually restoring the company's investment grade rating.

data.title: Consumer credit growth flat M/M in January
data.description:
• U.S. consumer borrowing was up $0.9B on a monthly basis in January 2025, compared with the $5.5B increase expected and the $40.8B rise in the prior month.
  • Total outstanding consumer credit stood at $4,989.8B, largely flat from $4,988.9B in December 2024, which was revised from $5,145B.
  • On a seasonally adjusted basis, consumer borrowing rose at an annual rate of 4.3%, the Federal Reserve said, with revolving credit advancing at an 8.2% annual rate and nonrevolving credit drifting up at a 3% annual rate.

data.title: Fed's Kugler expects monetary policy to stay in place 'for some time'
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kuglar said Friday that said the central bank’s monetary policy will likely stay in place “for some time” due to rising risks from inflation. 

“Given the recent increase in inflation expectations and the key inflation categories that have not shown progress toward our 2% target, it could be appropriate to continue holding the policy rate at its current level for some time,” she said in prepared remarks to the Banco de Portugal’s Conference on Monetary Policy Transmission and the Labor Market in Lisbon. 

“Going forward, I will continue to closely monitor the effects of policies on the economy, and I will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and any changes in the balance of risks,” she said.

The Federal Reserve’s rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee will hold its next meeting on March 18-19. The panel is nearly universally expected to keep the benchmark fed funds rate at between 4.25% and 4.5%, according to the CME Group’s FedWatch Tool. 

Kugler noted growing concerns over pressures on prices. 

“Notwithstanding the well-balanced labor market, there are important upside risks to inflation. Some measures of inflation expectations have risen significantly in the past couple of months,” she said.

According to Kugler, the job market in the U.S. is currently balanced and “that wages are a significant source of inflation pressure.”

“I am closely monitoring any signs of changes in the labor market so that we can keep it in the good place that it is now while bringing down inflation to our target,” she added.

data.title: Piper Sandler’s ‘actionable ideas’ – Buy NET, JD, SITM, Sell HAL
data.description:
Piper Sandler analysts presented their actionable stock recommendations, as the market engages in extreme bearish sentiment over concerns of economic growth, tariffs, and other policies.

Craig Johnson, chief market technician at Piper Sandler, noted that the investment company’s proprietary breadth indicators are in sell positions. “Our M.A.C.E. trend work (moving average cycle evaluation) reveals that more stocks are in downtrends than uptrends.”

However, he said the market’s downside will be limited, as he expects equity markets to pull in from their recent record highs toward key support levels.

“We suspect markets will find some footing as the S&P 500 (SP500) tests its 200-day moving average and the Russell 2000 (IWM) tests its 50% retracement from its October 2022 low,” he said. “A period of stabilization, reassessment, and rotation is likely at current levels.”

He added that some stabilization is due as the key long-term support is tested around the 5700 to 5800 level. The S&P 500 is currently at 5,762.

Here are Piper Sandler’s actionable ideas:

Cloudflare (NET)

The company’s shares are “retesting support from a prior breakout level” and are above their rising 30-week moving average. “Add to positions once support has been confirmed.”

JD.com (JD)

These shares are approaching the top of a bullish “cup and handle” formation and are back above their 10-week and 30-week moving average. “Add to positions on a breakout above resistance near $48.”

SiTime Corp. (SITM)

Here, shares have confirmed their support off a prior breakout level and relative strength has rebounded into positive territory. “A close above the 10-/30-week moving average should open the door to a new leg higher.”

Warner Bros Discovery (WBD)

The firm’s shares are approaching the neck of an “inverted head and shoulders” formation and are back above their 10-week and 30-week moving averages. Relative strength has risen to multi-year highs. “Add to positions on a breakout above the neckline near $12.70.”

Halliburton Co. (HAL)

Halliburton shares, however, are approaching their support off the 2022 lows and are back below their 10-week and 30-week moving averages. “Reduce positions on a violation of support.”
